skill.name: async-patterns-guide
skill.description: Guides Rust users through modern async patterns, including native async fn in traits, async closures, and when to avoid async-trait.
skill.summary: Helps modernize Rust async code with native trait and closure patterns.
skill.author: EmilLindfors

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported high-risk patterns, but review found false positives in Markdown fences, Rust examples, and descriptive skill text. No evidence found of executable scripts, shell command execution, network access, prompt injection, credential access, or data exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are Markdown code fence delimiters around Rust examples. The skill only allows Read and Grep and does not instruct the assistant to run external commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are Markdown fence markers, not Ruby backticks or shell execution. Surrounding content is explanatory Rust sample code with no command invocation.

low_findings.title: Database Method Names Misclassified as Sensitive Files
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The SQLite database findings point to Rust example calls on a database field. They do not reference a database file path, contain secrets, or request access to local data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is limited to illustrative Rust calls named query and insert. There is no SQLite file extension, credential, file path, or filesystem instruction.

low_findings.title: Skill Activation Text Misclassified as Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The line tells the assistant to recognize async Rust code and provide guidance. It does not collect host, network, identity, or environment information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text uses detect in the context of code review activation. No system discovery command or information-gathering workflow is present.

low_findings.title: Description Text Misclassified as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts point to the skill description, which discusses Rust async patterns. No hash, cipher, random number generation, or cryptographic API appears at this location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 contains only descriptive metadata about async traits and closures. I found no evidence of weak cryptographic code or recommendations.

content.value_statement: Rust async code can retain old dependency patterns after language support improves. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code suggest native async traits, async closures, and clear migration choices.
actual_capabilities: Identifies when native async fn in traits can replace async-trait.
Explains when async-trait remains useful for dyn Trait and object safety.
Shows migration paths from macro-based async traits to native Rust syntax.
Provides guidance for async closures using Rust 1.85 and AsyncFn bounds.
Compares static dispatch and dynamic dispatch tradeoffs for async code.
limitations: Does not run Rust compilers, tests, benchmarks, or dependency audits.
Does not support unsafe code review beyond async pattern guidance.
Requires the user or assistant to provide relevant Rust code context.
Assumes modern Rust versions when recommending native async features.

use_cases.title: Modernize Service Traits
use_cases.description: Review service and repository traits for native async fn opportunities.

use_cases.title: Plan Dependency Reduction
use_cases.description: Decide whether async-trait can be removed without breaking dynamic dispatch needs.

use_cases.title: Teach Async Tradeoffs
use_cases.description: Explain static dispatch, trait objects, and async closure patterns in practical terms.

prompt_templates.title: Check My Async Trait
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Rust trait and tell me whether native async fn is appropriate. Explain any version or dispatch requirements.

prompt_templates.title: Remove Async Trait Safely
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me migrate this async-trait usage to native Rust where possible. Keep dynamic dispatch cases unchanged.
prompt_templates.scenario: Dependency reduction in an existing codebase

prompt_templates.title: Compare Dispatch Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate these async APIs for static dispatch and dynamic dispatch. Recommend the simplest design that preserves behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Design Modern Async Interfaces
prompt_templates.prompt: Design async trait and async closure patterns for this Rust module. Include migration risks, MSRV assumptions, and performance tradeoffs.

output_examples.input: A repository trait uses async-trait, but callers use concrete generic types.
output_examples.output: The skill would recommend native async fn in the trait, explain the Rust 1.75 requirement, and note that static dispatch avoids macro boxing.

output_examples.input: A plugin registry stores many implementations behind trait objects.
output_examples.output: The skill would keep async-trait, because dyn Trait and object safety are required for that design.

output_examples.input: A batch processor accepts callbacks that perform async validation and saving.
output_examples.output: The skill would suggest an async closure interface with AsyncFn bounds when Rust 1.85 or newer is acceptable.

best_practices: State the minimum supported Rust version before recommending native async features.
Check whether dyn Trait is required before removing async-trait.
Explain performance implications in terms of boxing, vtables, and specialization.
anti_patterns: Removing async-trait from object-safe plugin APIs without redesigning dispatch.
Recommending native async closures when the project cannot use Rust 1.85.
Treating every async-trait dependency as unnecessary without checking callers.

faq.question: Does this skill execute code?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance from Rust source text and only declares Read and Grep as allowed tools.

faq.question: When should native async fn in traits be used?
faq.answer: Use it when static dispatch is acceptable and the project supports Rust 1.75 or newer.

faq.question: When is async-trait still useful?
faq.answer: Use async-trait when the design needs dyn Trait, object safety, or support for Rust versions before 1.75.

faq.question: Can it update my Rust code automatically?
faq.answer: The skill describes migration steps, but it does not request write tools or edit files by itself.

faq.question: Does it cover async closures?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for native async closures and AsyncFn bounds for Rust 1.85 or newer.

faq.question: Is it focused on performance?
faq.answer: Yes. It highlights static dispatch, dynamic dispatch, boxing overhead, and unnecessary macro dependencies.
